group.title: Delivery timetable and the cause of delay

proposition_text: Meridian did not at any time request any change to the agreed scope of the works, and all of the delay to the project was caused by TechFlow's failure to allocate adequate and competent resources.
summary: This pleaded allegation (paragraph 9 of Meridian's own Particulars of Claim, doc_002) is a conjunction of two limbs, and it fails. The first limb — that Meridian never requested any change to the agreed scope — is flatly contradicted by Meridian's own documents: the original SOW expressly excluded a customer loyalty module (doc_004), yet Meridian's Programme Director asked TechFlow to build one before go-live (doc_010), the parties signed Change Order No. 3 adding it 'at the Customer's request' and outside the original scope (doc_007), and Meridian's own witnesses Helena Vance (doc_016) and Priya Nair (doc_018) admit the request and that both scope and date were being changed; TechFlow's solicitors make the same point (doc_015). The second limb — that all of the delay to the project was caused by TechFlow's failure to allocate adequate and competent resources — is not addressed by any document in the bundle: no document evidences any inadequate or incompetent TechFlow resourcing, and the only documented cause of the schedule move from 1 October to 18 November 2024 is Meridian's own loyalty-module change order (doc_007, doc_010, doc_016, doc_018), while the post-go-live disruption is attributed to non-TechFlow causes such as a network outage at Meridian's own provider, in-store broadband, the Lutterworth distribution-centre flood and a soft retail market (doc_011, doc_012, doc_013, doc_017, doc_019, doc_020). Because one limb is contradicted and the other is wholly unproven, the conjunction fails and the overall judgement is adverse; the only document supporting the proposition is the pleading from which it is drawn (doc_002).

evidence.reasoning: The Claim Form gives only the brief details of claim (late and defective delivery under the MSA) and says nothing about whether Meridian requested a scope change or about the cause of any delay. It bears on neither the scope limb nor the resourcing-causation limb of the proposition.

This is the source pleading from which the proposition is drawn: paragraph 9 of the Particulars of Claim asserts both limbs almost verbatim. On its face it supports the proposition, but it is a self-serving allegation. The scope limb it pleads is contradicted by the rest of the bundle (see doc_004, doc_007, doc_010, doc_015, doc_016, doc_018), and the resourcing-causation limb it pleads is unsupported by any other document in the bundle.
evidence.excerpt: Meridian did not at any time request any change to the agreed scope of the works. All delay was caused by TechFlow’s failure to allocate adequate and competent resources to the project.

evidence.reasoning: The MSA's change-control regime undercuts the scope limb's framing. Clause 3.1 makes the go-live date a planning estimate adjustable through change control, and clause 3.2 provides that any change to scope, deliverables or timetable is agreed by signed Change Order — the very mechanism Meridian later used to add the loyalty module (doc_007). This contextualises that later change as a Meridian-requested scope variation. The MSA says nothing supporting the resourcing-causation limb.
evidence.excerpt: Any change to the scope, deliverables or timetable shall be agreed in writing by both parties by means of a signed Change Order. A signed Change Order varies this Agreement to the extent stated in it.

evidence.reasoning: The Statement of Work defines the 'agreed scope' and expressly excludes a customer loyalty module, and states the 1 October 2024 go-live is a target date subject to change control. This is the baseline against which Change Order No. 3 (doc_007) is a Meridian-requested scope change, directly contradicting the scope limb. It also undercuts any suggestion that the date was fixed such that the slip must be a TechFlow resourcing failure; it evidences no resourcing failure.
evidence.excerpt: The Supplier shall deliver the Platform in two phases: Phase 1 (core inventory and EPOS); Phase 2 (reporting and analytics). The agreed scope does not include a customer loyalty module.

evidence.reasoning: The Order Form records the Phase 1/Phase 2 line items and £2.4m total under the MSA. It confirms the original commercial scope (no loyalty-module line) but does not itself address whether Meridian requested a scope change or the cause of delay, so its bearing on either limb is marginal.
evidence.excerpt: Platform licence & implementation (Phase 1 — core inventory & EPOS) ... Reporting & analytics (Phase 2) ... Total (excl. VAT) 2,400,000

evidence.reasoning: Deed of Variation No. 1 varies only the payment milestone profile and expressly does not vary the scope of works or the go-live date. It neither supports nor contradicts the proposition directly, but it shows the parties distinguished carefully between payment-only variations and scope/timetable changes — context for the later loyalty-module change order (doc_007). It says nothing about resourcing.
evidence.excerpt: The parties wish to vary the payment milestone profile to reflect cash-flow phasing. This Deed does not vary the scope of the works or the target go-live date.

evidence.reasoning: Change Order No. 3 directly contradicts the scope limb: it records that, at the Customer's (Meridian's) request, TechFlow would build a loyalty module not in the original scope, signed for Meridian by Priya Nair (see doc_018), and that to accommodate the additional works the go-live date moved from 1 October to 18 November 2024. This shows Meridian did request a scope change and that the only documented cause of the project delay is that Meridian-requested change — not any TechFlow resourcing failure, which limb 2 alleges but no document supports.
evidence.excerpt: At the Customer’s request, the Supplier shall design, build and implement a customer loyalty module ... The Loyalty Module was not part of the original scope under SOW-01. ... the parties agree that, to accommodate the additional works, the go-live date is revised from 1 October 2024 to 18 November 2024.

evidence.reasoning: The UAT Acceptance Certificate (signed by Meridian on 12 November 2024) shows Phase 1 passed UAT with no outstanding Severity 1 or 2 items at that date. This cuts against any suggestion of a TechFlow performance or resourcing failure causing delay; it provides no support for the resourcing-causation limb, which remains unaddressed.
evidence.excerpt: Meridian confirms that Phase 1 has passed User Acceptance Testing and is accepted ... No outstanding Severity 1 or Severity 2 items were recorded at the date of this certificate.

evidence.reasoning: This go-live email thread is adverse to the causation limb. TechFlow recommended in writing deferring go-live to mid-January 2025, but Meridian's Programme Director rejected that advice and instructed go-live on 18 November, expressly accepting the risk. The timing decision flowed from Meridian's own choice, not from any TechFlow under-resourcing; the email evidences no resourcing failure.
evidence.excerpt: Our clear recommendation is to defer go-live to mid-January 2025 ... Please proceed to go-live on 18 November 2024 as planned. I understand your team’s reservations and I am content to take that risk on Meridian’s side.

evidence.reasoning: This email is direct documentary contradiction of the scope limb. Meridian's Programme Director Helena Vance asks TechFlow to add a customer loyalty capability before go-live, acknowledging it was not in the original SOW and accepting a possible knock-on to the go-live date; TechFlow then moves go-live from 1 October to 18 November. This is Meridian requesting a scope change that drove the timetable move — the documented cause of delay, not any TechFlow resourcing failure.
evidence.excerpt: the board wants a customer loyalty capability included in the platform before we go live ... I appreciate this was not in the original SOW ... we are willing to discuss a revised date to accommodate it.

evidence.reasoning: The 25 November outage root-cause email is adverse to the causation framing. TechFlow attributes the largest outage to loss of WAN connectivity at Meridian's data-centre provider (Northgate Telecom), not the Platform, while owning the stock-sync defects. It shows post-go-live disruption from a non-TechFlow cause and identifies no resourcing failure, so the resourcing-causation limb is not made out on this document.
evidence.excerpt: The root cause was a loss of wide-area network connectivity at your data centre provided by Northgate Telecom, not the Platform.

evidence.reasoning: This internal Meridian email is adverse to the causation framing. Meridian's own Head of Retail Operations attributes the poor December trading substantially to the Lutterworth distribution-centre flood and a soft market, warning against blaming the whole shortfall on the platform. It points to causes of disruption unconnected with TechFlow and identifies no resourcing failure, leaving the resourcing-causation limb unsupported.
evidence.excerpt: The Lutterworth distribution centre flooded on 3 December and was effectively out of action for around ten days ... This is unrelated to the platform. ... I’d be wary of attributing the whole shortfall to the platform when the DC flood and the wider market are doing a lot of the damage.

evidence.reasoning: The defect log is adverse to the causation framing. While it records genuine Platform stock-sync defects, it attributes the single largest outage (D-003) and the intermittent till freezes (D-006) to network connectivity provided by Meridian, not the Platform, and notes Platform-attributable unavailability was modest. It evidences no TechFlow resourcing failure, so the resourcing-causation limb is not supported.
evidence.excerpt: the single largest outage (D-003) and the intermittent till freezes (D-006) were attributable to network connectivity provided by Meridian, not to the Platform.

evidence.reasoning: Meridian's notice of termination / letter before action asserts late and defective delivery but does not address whether Meridian requested a scope change, and does not attribute delay to a TechFlow resourcing failure. It restates Meridian's adverse position generally but advances neither limb of the proposition, so it is essentially neutral.
evidence.excerpt: you delivered the Platform late; (ii) following go-live on 18 November 2024 the Platform suffered serious and repeated failures, including critical defects in the stock-synchronisation module

evidence.reasoning: TechFlow's response letter contradicts the scope limb directly and responds to the pleaded assertion in doc_002: it states Meridian's no-change assertion is wrong and is contradicted by its own signed Change Order, that the go-live moved by reason of Change Order No. 3 (the Meridian-requested loyalty module not in original scope), that TechFlow recommended deferral which Meridian rejected, and that major outages were due to Meridian's own network provider. It denies and provides no support for any resourcing failure.
evidence.excerpt: Your client’s assertion that it requested no change to scope is wrong and is contradicted by its own signed Change Order. ... Our client expressly recommended, in writing on 24 October 2024, that go-live be deferred ... Your client’s Programme Director rejected that recommendation and instructed our client to proceed on 18 November 2024, accepting the associated risk.

evidence.reasoning: Meridian's own Programme Director's witness statement contradicts the scope limb and responds to the pleaded case in doc_002. She admits she asked TechFlow to add the loyalty module, accepting it was not in the original SOW, documented in Change Order No. 3 (doc_007) which also moved go-live to 18 November; and she states she decided to go live despite TechFlow's written advice to delay (doc_009). This destroys the no-scope-change limb and shows the timing was Meridian's choice, not a TechFlow resourcing failure.
evidence.excerpt: I asked TechFlow to add a loyalty module. I accept this was not in the original Statement of Work. It was documented in Change Order No. 3 ... which also moved the go-live date to 18 November 2024. ... Marcus Reilly recommended in writing that we delay until January 2025 ... I took the decision to proceed despite TechFlow’s advice.

evidence.reasoning: Meridian's Head of Retail Operations' witness statement is adverse to the causation framing. He concedes a number of till outages were caused by in-store broadband and that the 25 November total outage was a network failure at Meridian's data-centre provider, and that poor December trading owed much to the Lutterworth flood and a weak market. These are non-TechFlow causes and he identifies no resourcing failure, so the resourcing-causation limb is left unaddressed.
evidence.excerpt: A number of the till outages in stores turned out to be caused by the broadband connections in the stores dropping out, rather than the platform itself. There was also a complete outage on 25 November that I am told was a network failure at our data centre provider, not the platform. ... the platform was not the only reason.

evidence.reasoning: Meridian's Head of Procurement's witness statement contradicts the scope limb. She confirms she signed Change Order No. 3 (doc_007) adding the customer loyalty module which Helena Vance (doc_016) had asked TechFlow to provide and which was not in the original SOW, revising go-live from 1 October to 18 November, and that she understood Meridian was changing both the scope and the date. This is a direct admission that Meridian requested a scope change and that the date move was caused by that change, not by resourcing.
evidence.excerpt: On 2 September 2024 I signed Change Order No. 3. This added the customer loyalty module, which Helena Vance had asked TechFlow to provide and which was not in the original Statement of Work, and it revised the go-live date from 1 October to 18 November 2024. I understood at the time that we were changing both the scope and the date.

evidence.reasoning: The IT expert (instructed by Meridian's own solicitors) is adverse to the causation framing. He finds Platform-attributable unavailability of only about 6.2%, the largest outage caused by Meridian's network provider, and shorter interruptions due to in-store broadband. Although he confirms genuine Severity 1 stock-sync defects fell below the expected standard, he attributes major disruption to non-Platform causes and identifies no TechFlow resourcing failure — directly leaving the resourcing-causation limb unproven.
evidence.excerpt: The single largest outage occurred on 25 November 2024 ... this outage was not caused by the Platform ... the cause was a loss of wide-area network connectivity at the Claimant’s data-centre provider (Northgate Telecom). A number of shorter in-store till interruptions were likewise attributable to in-store broadband, not to the Platform.

evidence.reasoning: The quantum expert is adverse to the causation framing. She attributes a material part of the Q4 shortfall to causes unconnected with the Platform — the Lutterworth DC flood and a sector-wide downturn evident before go-live — estimating roughly £1.5m of the claimed decline to non-Platform factors. This undermines the 'all caused by TechFlow' assertion and identifies no resourcing failure, so the resourcing-causation limb is not supported.
evidence.excerpt: A material part of the Q4 sales shortfall is, in my opinion, attributable to causes unconnected with the Platform. In particular: (a) the flooding of the Claimant’s Lutterworth distribution centre ... and (b) a sector-wide downturn in retail trading ... evident ... before go-live.
